sourceRefs.fallbackText: 8. Errors and Exceptions
sourceRefs.textQuote: 8. Errors and Exceptions

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Theme Auto Light Dark |
sourceRefs.textQuote: Theme Auto Light Dark |

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Until now error messages haven’t been more than mentioned, but if you have tried out the examples you have probably seen some. There are (at least) two distinguishable kinds of errors: syntax errors and exceptions .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Until now error messages haven’t been more than mentioned, but if you have tried out the examples you have probably seen some. There are (at least) two distinguishable kinds of errors: syntax errors and exceptions .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Syntax errors, also known as parsing errors, are perhaps the most common kind of complaint you get while you are still learning Python:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Syntax errors, also known as parsing errors, are perhaps the most common kind of complaint you get while you are still learning Python:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> while True print ( 'Hello world' ) File "<stdin>" , line 1 while True print ( 'Hello world' ) ^^^^^ SyntaxError : invalid syntax
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> while True print ( 'Hello world' ) File "<stdin>" , line 1 while True print ( 'Hello world' ) ^^^^^ SyntaxError : invalid syntax

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The parser repeats the offending line and displays little arrows pointing at the place where the error was detected. Note that this is not always the place that needs to be fixed. In the example, the error is detected at the function print() , since a colon ( ':' ) is missing just before it.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The parser repeats the offending line and displays little arrows pointing at the place where the error was detected. Note that this is not always the place that needs to be fixed. In the example, the error is detected at

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The file name ( <stdin> in our example) and line number are printed so you know where to look in case the input came from a file.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The file name ( <stdin> in our example) and line number are printed so you know where to look in case the input came from a file.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Even if a statement or expression is syntactically correct, it may cause an error when an attempt is made to execute it. Errors detected during execution are called exceptions and are not unconditionally fatal: you will soon learn how to handle them in Python programs. Most exceptions are not handled by programs, however, and result in error messages as show
sourceRefs.textQuote: Even if a statement or expression is syntactically correct, it may cause an error when an attempt is made to execute it. Errors detected during execution are called exceptions and are not unconditionally fatal: you will

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> 10 * ( 1 / 0 ) Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> 10 * ( 1 / 0 ) ~^~ ZeroDivisionError : division by zero >>> 4 + spam * 3 Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> 4 + spam * 3 ^^^^ NameError : name 'spam' is not defined >>> '2' + 2 Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , l
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> 10 * ( 1 / 0 ) Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> 10 * ( 1 / 0 ) ~^~ ZeroDivisionError : division by zero >>> 4 + spam * 3 Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , lin

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The last line of the error message indicates what happened. Exceptions come in different types, and the type is printed as part of the message: the types in the example are ZeroDivisionError , NameError and TypeError . The string printed as the exception type is the name of the built-in exception that occurred. This is true for all built-in exceptions, but n
sourceRefs.textQuote: The last line of the error message indicates what happened. Exceptions come in different types, and the type is printed as part of the message: the types in the example are ZeroDivisionError , NameError and TypeError . T

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The rest of the line provides detail based on the type of exception and what caused it.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The rest of the line provides detail based on the type of exception and what caused it.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The preceding part of the error message shows the context where the exception occurred, in the form of a stack traceback. In general it contains a stack traceback listing source lines; however, it will not display lines read from standard input.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The preceding part of the error message shows the context where the exception occurred, in the form of a stack traceback. In general it contains a stack traceback listing source lines; however, it will not display lines

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Built-in Exceptions lists the built-in exceptions and their meanings.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Built-in Exceptions lists the built-in exceptions and their meanings.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is possible to write programs that handle selected exceptions. Look at the following example, which asks the user for input until a valid integer has been entered, but allows the user to interrupt the program (using Control - C or whatever the operating system supports); note that a user-generated interruption is signalled by raising the KeyboardInterrupt
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is possible to write programs that handle selected exceptions. Look at the following example, which asks the user for input until a valid integer has been entered, but allows the user to interrupt the program (using C

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> while True : ... try : ... x = int ( input ( "Please enter a number: " )) ... break ... except ValueError : ... print ( "Oops! That was no valid number. Try again..." ) ...
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> while True : ... try : ... x = int ( input ( "Please enter a number: " )) ... break ... except ValueError : ... print ( "Oops! That was no valid number. Try again..." ) ...

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The try statement works as follows.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The try statement works as follows.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: First, the try clause (the statement(s) between the try and except keywords) is executed.
sourceRefs.textQuote: First, the try clause (the statement(s) between the try and except keywords) is executed.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If no exception occurs, the except clause is skipped and execution of the try statement is finished.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If no exception occurs, the except clause is skipped and execution of the try statement is finished.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If an exception occurs during execution of the try clause, the rest of the clause is skipped. Then, if its type matches the exception named after the except keyword, the except clause is executed, and then execution continues after the try/except block.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If an exception occurs during execution of the try clause, the rest of the clause is skipped. Then, if its type matches the exception named after the except keyword, the except clause is executed, and then execution cont

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If an exception occurs which does not match the exception named in the except clause , it is passed on to outer try statements; if no handler is found, it is an unhandled exception and execution stops with an error message.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If an exception occurs which does not match the exception named in the except clause , it is passed on to outer try statements; if no handler is found, it is an unhandled exception and execution stops with an error messa

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A try statement may have more than one except clause , to specify handlers for different exceptions. At most one handler will be executed. Handlers only handle exceptions that occur in the corresponding try clause , not in other handlers of the same try statement. An except clause may name multiple exceptions, for example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A try statement may have more than one except clause , to specify handlers for different exceptions. At most one handler will be executed. Handlers only handle exceptions that occur in the corresponding try clause , not

sourceRefs.fallbackText: ... except RuntimeError , TypeError , NameError : ... pass
sourceRefs.textQuote: ... except RuntimeError , TypeError , NameError : ... pass

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A class in an except clause matches exceptions which are instances of the class itself or one of its derived classes (but not the other way around — an except clause listing a derived class does not match instances of its base classes). For example, the following code will print B, C, D in that order:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A class in an except clause matches exceptions which are instances of the class itself or one of its derived classes (but not the other way around — an except clause listing a derived class does not match instances of it

sourceRefs.fallbackText: class B ( Exception ): pass class C ( B ): pass class D ( C ): pass for cls in [ B , C , D ]: try : raise cls () except D : print ( "D" ) except C : print ( "C" ) except B : print ( "B" )
sourceRefs.textQuote: class B ( Exception ): pass class C ( B ): pass class D ( C ): pass for cls in [ B , C , D ]: try : raise cls () except D : print ( "D" ) except C : print ( "C" ) except B : print ( "B" )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that if the except clauses were reversed (with except B first), it would have printed B, B, B — the first matching except clause is triggered.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note that if the except clauses were reversed (with except B first), it would have printed B, B, B — the first matching except clause is triggered.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When an exception occurs, it may have associated values, also known as the exception’s arguments . The presence and types of the arguments depend on the exception type.
sourceRefs.textQuote: When an exception occurs, it may have associated values, also known as the exception’s arguments . The presence and types of the arguments depend on the exception type.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The except clause may specify a variable after the exception name. The variable is bound to the exception instance which typically has an args attribute that stores the arguments. For convenience, builtin exception types define __str__() to print all the arguments without explicitly accessing .args .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The except clause may specify a variable after the exception name. The variable is bound to the exception instance which typically has an args attribute that stores the arguments. For convenience, builtin exception types

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> try : ... raise Exception ( 'spam' , 'eggs' ) ... except Exception as inst : ... print ( type ( inst )) # the exception type ... print ( inst . args ) # arguments stored in .args ... print ( inst ) # __str__ allows args to be printed directly, ... # but may be overridden in exception subclasses ... x , y = inst . args # unpack args ... print ( 'x =' , x
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> try : ... raise Exception ( 'spam' , 'eggs' ) ... except Exception as inst : ... print ( type ( inst )) # the exception type ... print ( inst . args ) # arguments stored in .args ... print ( inst ) # __str__ allows a

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The exception’s __str__() output is printed as the last part (‘detail’) of the message for unhandled exceptions.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The exception’s __str__() output is printed as the last part (‘detail’) of the message for unhandled exceptions.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: BaseException is the common base class of all exceptions. One of its subclasses, Exception , is the base class of all the non-fatal exceptions. Exceptions which are not subclasses of Exception are not typically handled, because they are used to indicate that the program should terminate. They include SystemExit which is raised by sys.exit() and KeyboardInter
sourceRefs.textQuote: BaseException is the common base class of all exceptions. One of its subclasses, Exception , is the base class of all the non-fatal exceptions. Exceptions which are not subclasses of Exception are not typically handled,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Exception can be used as a wildcard that catches (almost) everything. However, it is good practice to be as specific as possible with the types of exceptions that we intend to handle, and to allow any unexpected exceptions to propagate on.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Exception can be used as a wildcard that catches (almost) everything. However, it is good practice to be as specific as possible with the types of exceptions that we intend to handle, and to allow any unexpected exceptio

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The most common pattern for handling Exception is to print or log the exception and then re-raise it (allowing a caller to handle the exception as well):
sourceRefs.textQuote: The most common pattern for handling Exception is to print or log the exception and then re-raise it (allowing a caller to handle the exception as well):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: import sys try : f = open ( 'myfile.txt' ) s = f . readline () i = int ( s . strip ()) except OSError as err : print ( "OS error:" , err ) except ValueError : print ( "Could not convert data to an integer." ) except Exception as err : print ( f "Unexpected { err =} , { type ( err ) =} " ) raise
sourceRefs.textQuote: import sys try : f = open ( 'myfile.txt' ) s = f . readline () i = int ( s . strip ()) except OSError as err : print ( "OS error:" , err ) except ValueError : print ( "Could not convert data to an integer." ) except Exce

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The try … except statement has an optional else clause , which, when present, must follow all except clauses . It is useful for code that must be executed if the try clause does not raise an exception. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The try … except statement has an optional else clause , which, when present, must follow all except clauses . It is useful for code that must be executed if the try clause does not raise an exception. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: for arg in sys . argv [ 1 :]: try : f = open ( arg , 'r' ) except OSError : print ( 'cannot open' , arg ) else : print ( arg , 'has' , len ( f . readlines ()), 'lines' ) f . close ()
sourceRefs.textQuote: for arg in sys . argv [ 1 :]: try : f = open ( arg , 'r' ) except OSError : print ( 'cannot open' , arg ) else : print ( arg , 'has' , len ( f . readlines ()), 'lines' ) f . close ()

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The use of the else clause is better than adding additional code to the try clause because it avoids accidentally catching an exception that wasn’t raised by the code being protected by the try … except statement.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The use of the else clause is better than adding additional code to the try clause because it avoids accidentally catching an exception that wasn’t raised by the code being protected by the try … except statement.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Exception handlers do not handle only exceptions that occur immediately in the try clause , but also those that occur inside functions that are called (even indirectly) in the try clause . For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Exception handlers do not handle only exceptions that occur immediately in the try clause , but also those that occur inside functions that are called (even indirectly) in the try clause . For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> def this_fails (): ... x = 1 / 0 ... >>> try : ... this_fails () ... except ZeroDivisionError as err : ... print ( 'Handling run-time error:' , err ) ... Handling run-time error: division by zero
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> def this_fails (): ... x = 1 / 0 ... >>> try : ... this_fails () ... except ZeroDivisionError as err : ... print ( 'Handling run-time error:' , err ) ... Handling run-time error: division by zero

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The raise statement allows the programmer to force a specified exception to occur. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The raise statement allows the programmer to force a specified exception to occur. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> raise NameError ( 'HiThere' ) Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> raise NameError ( 'HiThere' ) NameError : HiThere
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> raise NameError ( 'HiThere' ) Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> raise NameError ( 'HiThere' ) NameError : HiThere

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The sole argument to raise indicates the exception to be raised. This must be either an exception instance or an exception class (a class that derives from BaseException , such as Exception or one of its subclasses). If an exception class is passed, it will be implicitly instantiated by calling its constructor with no arguments:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The sole argument to raise indicates the exception to be raised. This must be either an exception instance or an exception class (a class that derives from BaseException , such as Exception or one of its subclasses). If

sourceRefs.fallbackText: raise ValueError # shorthand for 'raise ValueError()'
sourceRefs.textQuote: raise ValueError # shorthand for 'raise ValueError()'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you need to determine whether an exception was raised but don’t intend to handle it, a simpler form of the raise statement allows you to re-raise the exception:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you need to determine whether an exception was raised but don’t intend to handle it, a simpler form of the raise statement allows you to re-raise the exception:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> try : ... raise NameError ( 'HiThere' ) ... except NameError : ... print ( 'An exception flew by!' ) ... raise ... An exception flew by! Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 2 , in <module> raise NameError ( 'HiThere' ) NameError : HiThere
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> try : ... raise NameError ( 'HiThere' ) ... except NameError : ... print ( 'An exception flew by!' ) ... raise ... An exception flew by! Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 2 , in <module> raise

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If an unhandled exception occurs inside an except section, it will have the exception being handled attached to it and included in the error message:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If an unhandled exception occurs inside an except section, it will have the exception being handled attached to it and included in the error message:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> try : ... open ( "database.sqlite" ) ... except OSError : ... raise RuntimeError ( "unable to handle error" ) ... Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 2 , in <module> open ( "database.sqlite" ) ~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ FileNotFoundError : [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'database.sqlite' During handling of the above exception, ano
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> try : ... open ( "database.sqlite" ) ... except OSError : ... raise RuntimeError ( "unable to handle error" ) ... Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 2 , in <module> open ( "database.sqlite" ) ~~

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To indicate that an exception is a direct consequence of another, the raise statement allows an optional from clause:
sourceRefs.textQuote: To indicate that an exception is a direct consequence of another, the raise statement allows an optional from clause:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: # exc must be exception instance or None. raise RuntimeError from exc
sourceRefs.textQuote: # exc must be exception instance or None. raise RuntimeError from exc

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This can be useful when you are transforming exceptions. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This can be useful when you are transforming exceptions. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> def func (): ... raise ConnectionError ... >>> try : ... func () ... except ConnectionError as exc : ... raise RuntimeError ( 'Failed to open database' ) from exc ... Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 2 , in <module> func () ~~~~^^ File "<stdin>" , line 2 , in func ConnectionError The above exception was the direct cause of the fol
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> def func (): ... raise ConnectionError ... >>> try : ... func () ... except ConnectionError as exc : ... raise RuntimeError ( 'Failed to open database' ) from exc ... Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>"

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It also allows disabling automatic exception chaining using the from None idiom:
sourceRefs.textQuote: It also allows disabling automatic exception chaining using the from None idiom:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> try : ... open ( 'database.sqlite' ) ... except OSError : ... raise RuntimeError from None ... Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 4 , in <module> raise RuntimeError from None RuntimeError
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> try : ... open ( 'database.sqlite' ) ... except OSError : ... raise RuntimeError from None ... Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 4 , in <module> raise RuntimeError from None RuntimeError

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For more information about chaining mechanics, see Built-in Exceptions .
sourceRefs.textQuote: For more information about chaining mechanics, see Built-in Exceptions .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Programs may name their own exceptions by creating a new exception class (see Classes for more about Python classes). Exceptions should typically be derived from the Exception class, either directly or indirectly.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Programs may name their own exceptions by creating a new exception class (see Classes for more about Python classes). Exceptions should typically be derived from the Exception class, either directly or indirectly.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Exception classes can be defined which do anything any other class can do, but are usually kept simple, often only offering a number of attributes that allow information about the error to be extracted by handlers for the exception.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Exception classes can be defined which do anything any other class can do, but are usually kept simple, often only offering a number of attributes that allow information about the error to be extracted by handlers for th

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Most exceptions are defined with names that end in “Error”, similar to the naming of the standard exceptions.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Most exceptions are defined with names that end in “Error”, similar to the naming of the standard exceptions.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Many standard modules define their own exceptions to report errors that may occur in functions they define.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Many standard modules define their own exceptions to report errors that may occur in functions they define.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The try statement has another optional clause which is intended to define clean-up actions that must be executed under all circumstances. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The try statement has another optional clause which is intended to define clean-up actions that must be executed under all circumstances. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> try : ... raise KeyboardInterrupt ... finally : ... print ( 'Goodbye, world!' ) ... Goodbye, world! Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 2 , in <module> raise KeyboardInterrupt KeyboardInterrupt
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> try : ... raise KeyboardInterrupt ... finally : ... print ( 'Goodbye, world!' ) ... Goodbye, world! Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 2 , in <module> raise KeyboardInterrupt KeyboardInterrupt

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If a finally clause is present, the finally clause will execute as the last task before the try statement completes. The finally clause runs whether or not the try statement produces an exception. The following points discuss more complex cases when an exception occurs:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If a finally clause is present, the finally clause will execute as the last task before the try statement completes. The finally clause runs whether or not the try statement produces an exception. The following points di

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If an exception occurs during execution of the try clause, the exception may be handled by an except clause. If the exception is not handled by an except clause, the exception is re-raised after the finally clause has been executed.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If an exception occurs during execution of the try clause, the exception may be handled by an except clause. If the exception is not handled by an except clause, the exception is re-raised after the finally clause has be

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An exception could occur during execution of an except or else clause. Again, the exception is re-raised after the finally clause has been executed.
sourceRefs.textQuote: An exception could occur during execution of an except or else clause. Again, the exception is re-raised after the finally clause has been executed.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the finally clause executes a break , continue or return statement, exceptions are not re-raised. This can be confusing and is therefore discouraged. From version 3.14 the compiler emits a SyntaxWarning for it (see PEP 765 ).
sourceRefs.textQuote: If the finally clause executes a break , continue or return statement, exceptions are not re-raised. This can be confusing and is therefore discouraged. From version 3.14 the compiler emits a SyntaxWarning for it (see PE

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the try statement reaches a break , continue or return statement, the finally clause will execute just prior to the break , continue or return statement’s execution.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If the try statement reaches a break , continue or return statement, the finally clause will execute just prior to the break , continue or return statement’s execution.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If a finally clause includes a return statement, the returned value will be the one from the finally clause’s return statement, not the value from the try clause’s return statement. This can be confusing and is therefore discouraged. From version 3.14 the compiler emits a SyntaxWarning for it (see PEP 765 ).
sourceRefs.textQuote: If a finally clause includes a return statement, the returned value will be the one from the finally clause’s return statement, not the value from the try clause’s return statement. This can be confusing and is therefore

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> def bool_return (): ... try : ... return True ... finally : ... return False ... >>> bool_return () False
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> def bool_return (): ... try : ... return True ... finally : ... return False ... >>> bool_return () False

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A more complicated example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A more complicated example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> def divide ( x , y ): ... try : ... result = x / y ... except ZeroDivisionError : ... print ( "division by zero!" ) ... else : ... print ( "result is" , result ) ... finally : ... print ( "executing finally clause" ) ... >>> divide ( 2 , 1 ) result is 2.0 executing finally clause >>> divide ( 2 , 0 ) division by zero! executing finally clause >>> divide
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> def divide ( x , y ): ... try : ... result = x / y ... except ZeroDivisionError : ... print ( "division by zero!" ) ... else : ... print ( "result is" , result ) ... finally : ... print ( "executing finally clause" )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As you can see, the finally clause is executed in any event. The TypeError raised by dividing two strings is not handled by the except clause and therefore re-raised after the finally clause has been executed.
sourceRefs.textQuote: As you can see, the finally clause is executed in any event. The TypeError raised by dividing two strings is not handled by the except clause and therefore re-raised after the finally clause has been executed.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In real world applications, the finally clause is useful for releasing external resources (such as files or network connections), regardless of whether the use of the resource was successful.
sourceRefs.textQuote: In real world applications, the finally clause is useful for releasing external resources (such as files or network connections), regardless of whether the use of the resource was successful.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Some objects define standard clean-up actions to be undertaken when the object is no longer needed, regardless of whether or not the operation using the object succeeded or failed. Look at the following example, which tries to open a file and print its contents to the screen.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Some objects define standard clean-up actions to be undertaken when the object is no longer needed, regardless of whether or not the operation using the object succeeded or failed. Look at the following example, which tr

sourceRefs.fallbackText: for line in open ( "myfile.txt" ): print ( line , end = "" )
sourceRefs.textQuote: for line in open ( "myfile.txt" ): print ( line , end = "" )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The problem with this code is that it leaves the file open for an indeterminate amount of time after this part of the code has finished executing. This is not an issue in simple scripts, but can be a problem for larger applications. The with statement allows objects like files to be used in a way that ensures they are always cleaned up promptly and correctly
sourceRefs.textQuote: The problem with this code is that it leaves the file open for an indeterminate amount of time after this part of the code has finished executing. This is not an issue in simple scripts, but can be a problem for larger a

sourceRefs.fallbackText: with open ( "myfile.txt" ) as f : for line in f : print ( line , end = "" )
sourceRefs.textQuote: with open ( "myfile.txt" ) as f : for line in f : print ( line , end = "" )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: After the statement is executed, the file f is always closed, even if a problem was encountered while processing the lines. Objects which, like files, provide predefined clean-up actions will indicate this in their documentation.
sourceRefs.textQuote: After the statement is executed, the file f is always closed, even if a problem was encountered while processing the lines. Objects which, like files, provide predefined clean-up actions will indicate this in their docum

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are situations where it is necessary to report several exceptions that have occurred. This is often the case in concurrency frameworks, when several tasks may have failed in parallel, but there are also other use cases where it is desirable to continue execution and collect multiple errors rather than raise the first exception.
headingPath: 8.9. Raising and Handling Multiple Unrelated Exceptions ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: There are situations where it is necessary to report several exceptions that have occurred. This is often the case in concurrency frameworks, when several tasks may have failed in parallel, but there are also other use c

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The builtin ExceptionGroup wraps a list of exception instances so that they can be raised together. It is an exception itself, so it can be caught like any other exception.
headingPath: 8.9. Raising and Handling Multiple Unrelated Exceptions ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The builtin ExceptionGroup wraps a list of exception instances so that they can be raised together. It is an exception itself, so it can be caught like any other exception.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> def f (): ... excs = [ OSError ( 'error 1' ), SystemError ( 'error 2' )] ... raise ExceptionGroup ( 'there were problems' , excs ) ... >>> f () + Exception Group Traceback (most recent call last): | File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module> | f() | ~^^ | File "<stdin>", line 3, in f | raise ExceptionGroup('there were problems', excs) | ExceptionGroup: there we

sourceRefs.fallbackText: By using except* instead of except , we can selectively handle only the exceptions in the group that match a certain type. In the following example, which shows a nested exception group, each except* clause extracts from the group exceptions of a certain type while letting all other exceptions propagate to other clauses and eventually to be reraised.
headingPath: 8.9. Raising and Handling Multiple Unrelated Exceptions ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: By using except* instead of except , we can selectively handle only the exceptions in the group that match a certain type. In the following example, which shows a nested exception group, each except* clause extracts from

RecursionError ( 4 ) ... ] ... ) ... ] ... ) ... >>> try : ... f () ... except * OSError as e : ... print ( "There were OSErrors" ) ... except * SystemError as e : ... print ( "There were Sys

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that the exceptions nested in an exception group must be instances, not types. This is because in practice the exceptions would typically be ones that have already been raised and caught by the program, along the following pattern:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> excs = [] ... for test in tests : ... try : ... test . run () ... except Exception as e : ... excs . append ( e ) ... >>> if excs : ... raise ExceptionGroup ( "Test Failures" , excs ) ...

sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> excs = [] ... for test in tests : ... try : ... test . run () ... except Exception as e : ... excs . append ( e ) ... >>> if excs : ... raise ExceptionGroup ( "Test Failures" , excs ) ...

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When an exception is created in order to be raised, it is usually initialized with information that describes the error that has occurred. There are cases where it is useful to add information after the exception was caught. For this purpose, exceptions have a method add_note(note) that accepts a string and adds it to the exception’s notes list. The standard
sourceRefs.textQuote: When an exception is created in order to be raised, it is usually initialized with information that describes the error that has occurred. There are cases where it is useful to add information after the exception was cau

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For example, when collecting exceptions into an exception group, we may want to add context information for the individual errors. In the following each exception in the group has a note indicating when this error has occurred.
sourceRefs.textQuote: For example, when collecting exceptions into an exception group, we may want to add context information for the individual errors. In the following each exception in the group has a note indicating when this error has oc

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> def f (): ... raise OSError ( 'operation failed' ) ... >>> excs = [] >>> for i in range ( 3 ): ... try : ... f () ... except Exception as e : ... e . add_note ( f 'Happened in Iteration { i + 1 } ' ) ... excs . append ( e ) ... >>> raise ExceptionGroup ( 'We have some problems' , excs ) + Exception Group Traceback (most recent call last): | File "<stdin>
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> def f (): ... raise OSError ( 'operation failed' ) ... >>> excs = [] >>> for i in range ( 3 ): ... try : ... f () ... except Exception as e : ... e . add_note ( f 'Happened in Iteration { i + 1 } ' ) ... excs . appen
